perturbation.original_text: 4. No Proprietary Information shall be disclosed to any third party whatsoever by RECIPIENT without the prior written approval of INFE and all such information shall be protected with at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that Recipient uses to protect its own Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. No Proprietary Information shall be disclosed to any third party whatsoever by RECIPIENT without the prior written approval of INFE and all such information shall be protected with reasonable diligence, care, and precaution.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase "reasonable diligence, care, and precaution" is ambiguous and weakens the obligation to protect proprietary information to the same standard as the recipient's own, potentially leading to inadequate protection and disclosure. It could mean a lower standard of care than what the recipient actually uses to protect its own information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-337 states that to maintain trade secret protection, the owner must have taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret. Replacing 'the same diligence, care, and precaution that Recipient uses to protect its own Proprietary Information' with 'reasonable diligence, care, and precaution' creates an ambiguity as to whether sufficient steps have been taken to protect the trade secrets, potentially failing to meet the "reasonable measures" standard required under Virginia law for trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 59.1-337. Injunctive relief LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce » Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act » § 59.1-337. Injunctive relief Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 7/13/2025 § 59.1-337 . Injunctive relief. A. Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. B. In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. C. In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. 1986, c. 210. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. Virginia Law Library The Code of Virginia, Constitution of Virginia, Charters, Authorities, Compacts and Unc
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the recipient to protect proprietary information with 'the same diligence, care, and precaution' they use for their own information. The changed text weakens this by requiring only 'reasonable diligence, care, and precaution.' According to the Law Explanation, Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-337 requires 'reasonable measures' to protect trade secrets. The Perturbation Explanation also notes that the change could result in a lower standard of care. While the Law Explanation argues the change creates an ambiguity, the changed text introduces a subjective standard (reasonableness) that could be interpreted as less protective than the original standard, which was tied to the recipient's own practices. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows § 59.1-337 addresses injunctive relief, which implies a legal standard of protection for trade secrets. The modified text, therefore, could be seen as contradicting the necessary level of protection required to prevent misappropriation, because 'reasonable' is open to interpretation and could potentially be lower than what the recipient actually uses to protect its own information. This undermines the effort to ensure at least an equivalent standard of care.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, the parties desire to protect and maintain the confidentiality of their respective trade secrets and/or other proprietary information (hereafter collectively referred to as "Proprietary Information") and to control and restrict its dissemination to prevent disclosure to unauthorized parties; and
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, the parties desire to generally maintain the confidentiality of their respective trade secrets and/or other proprietary information (hereafter collectively referred to as "Proprietary Information") and to control and restrict its excessive dissemination to prevent unwarranted disclosure to unauthorized parties; and
perturbation.explanation: The use of "generally maintain," "excessive dissemination," and "unwarranted disclosure" introduces ambiguity. 'Generally maintain' weakens the duty of confidentiality, while 'excessive dissemination' and 'unwarranted disclosure' make the restriction subjective, potentially conflicting with the level of protection required by Virginia law for trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336 defines 'trade secret' and implies a need for consistent and diligent protection. The changes introduce ambiguity, making the required level of protection uncertain, potentially leading to a situation where the measures taken are not considered 'reasonable' under the statute, thus jeopardizing trade secret status.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 59.1-336. Short title and definitions LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce » Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act » § 59.1-336. Short title and definitions Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 7/13/2025 § 59.1-336 . Short title and definitions. As used in this chapter, which may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, use of a computer or computer network without authority, breach of a duty or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: 1. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or 2. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (1) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (2) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (4) Acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity in the confidentiality obligations, which contradicts the need for consistent and diligent protection of trade secrets as implied by Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336. The original text aims to 'protect and maintain the confidentiality' and 'restrict its dissemination', while the changed text weakens this by stating the parties desire to 'generally maintain' and 'restrict its excessive dissemination,' making the required level of protection uncertain. The law snippet defines 'misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, and the changes in the contract introduce subjectivity which could make it difficult to prove misappropriation, jeopardizing trade secret status.

perturbation.original_text: 1. INFE will disclose to RECIPIENT such portions of its Proprietary Information solely to enable RECIPIENT to determine the desirability of selling its assets to INFE; and RECIPIENT shall not disseminate any portions of the Proprietary Information to any third party individual or entity.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. INFE will disclose to RECIPIENT such portions of its Proprietary Information solely to enable RECIPIENT to consider the possible desirability of potentially selling its assets to INFE; and RECIPIENT shall endeavor to not disseminate Proprietary Information to third parties, except where reasonably necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'determine the desirability' to 'consider the possible desirability of potentially selling' weakens the purpose of the disclosure, making it less definitive. Introducing "endeavor to not disseminate" and "except where reasonably necessary" creates exceptions to the non-dissemination clause that are not clearly defined, potentially allowing for unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The open-ended exception of 'except where reasonably necessary' directly contradicts the level of protection the law requires for a trade secret to retain its protection under the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The change may lead to dissemination which would not be acceptable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity in the non-dissemination clause through the phrase 'except where reasonably necessary,' which directly contradicts the level of protection required for trade secrets under the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By allowing dissemination where 'reasonably necessary,' the changed text weakens the protection of proprietary information and creates a loophole for potential misappropriation, directly undermining the confidentiality required to maintain trade secret status under the law.

perturbation.original_text: and all such information shall be protected with at least the same diligence, care, and precaution that Recipient uses to protect its own Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: and such information should be protected with diligence, care, and precaution.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of "at least the same...that Recipient uses to protect its own Proprietary Information" removes the clear benchmark for the level of protection required and weakens the protection, possibly to a level below what is needed to protect the information effectively, and that is required by state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336 requires "reasonable efforts" to protect trade secrets. Removing the comparison to the recipient's own proprietary information protections introduces uncertainty about the adequacy of the protective measures, and whether these measures would be "reasonable." This could jeopardize trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text provided a clear benchmark for protecting information, linking it to the recipient's own proprietary information protection standards. The changed text removes this benchmark, introducing ambiguity. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that this introduces uncertainty about whether the protective measures would be "reasonable", potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under Va. Code Ann. § 59.1-336. Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' and 'misappropriation', which directly relates to the protection of trade secrets. By removing the explicit standard, the changed text weakens the obligation to maintain secrecy, potentially leading to misappropriation if the level of care falls below what is reasonable.

perturbation.original_text: 8. With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire three (3) years from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than three (3) years.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. With respect to each item of Proprietary Information disclosed, this obligation of the parties under this Agreement shall expire approximately three (3) years from the date of each such disclosure or the maximum period permitted by law if such period is less than three (3) years, unless otherwise agreed upon.
perturbation.explanation: By adding "approximately" and "unless otherwise agreed upon," the expiration date of the confidentiality obligation becomes vague and subject to change, contradicting the fixed period of protection typically required for trade secrets under Virginia law. This undermines the certainty required for enforceable trade secret agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act doesn't explicitly state a mandatory duration for confidentiality, the nature of trade secret protection implies a reasonable and definite period. By adding "approximately" and "unless otherwise agreed upon," the term of protection becomes indefinite, which can weaken the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement and potentially result in a loss of trade secret status if the protection is deemed insufficient.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the expiration date of the confidentiality obligation by adding "approximately" and "unless otherwise agreed upon." This contradicts the need for a definite period of protection for trade secrets, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly state a mandatory duration, it defines 'Misappropriation' and refers to 'a duty to maintain secrecy,' implying a reasonably definite period. The added ambiguity weakens the enforceability of the agreement and could lead to the loss of trade secret status, as stated in the Law Explanation. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.
